adipose_tissue: Histology image showing adipose tissue, composed of clusters of fat cells with clear cytoplasm.
Adipose tissue appears as large, round cells with thin cytoplasmic rims and eccentric nuclei.
The image displays well-defined lobules of adipocytes separated by thin connective tissue septa.
Adipose tissue serves as an energy store and provides insulation and cushioning in the body.
This class is characterized by the presence of abundant lipid droplets within the cells.
complex_stroma: Histology image showing complex stroma, consisting of dense connective tissue with a heterogeneous cellular composition.
Complex stroma contains a mixture of fibroblasts, immune cells, and extracellular matrix components.
The image may reveal irregular, fibrous areas with variable cellularity and collagen deposition.
Complex stroma often supports tumor growth and can influence the tumor microenvironment.
This class is distinguished by its intricate structure and diverse cell types.
debris: Histology image showing debris, consisting of fragmented cellular material and necrotic tissue.
Debris appears as amorphous, granular material lacking organized structure.
The image may reveal remnants of dead cells, nuclear fragments, and proteinaceous material.
Debris is often found in areas of tissue breakdown, inflammation, or necrosis.
This class is characterized by the absence of intact cells and the presence of disorganized material.
empty_background: Histology image showing empty background, with no significant cellular or tissue structures present.
The image appears clear or lightly stained, lacking identifiable anatomical features.
Empty background regions serve as negative space between tissue elements on the slide.
There are no cells, stroma, or debris in these areas, providing contrast for tissue identification.
This class is defined by the absence of any histological content.
immune_cells: Histology image showing immune cells, such as lymphocytes, plasma cells, or macrophages.
Immune cells appear as small, round cells with dense nuclei and scant cytoplasm.
The image may reveal clusters or scattered immune cells within the stroma or around glands.
Immune cells play a key role in inflammation, defense, and tumor surveillance.
This class is characterized by the presence of numerous, darkly stained nuclei.
normal_mucosal_glands: Histology image showing normal mucosal glands, with well-organized, tubular structures lined by epithelial cells.
Normal mucosal glands appear uniform in size and shape, with regular, basally located nuclei.
The image displays intact glandular architecture and absence of dysplasia or neoplasia.
Normal mucosal glands are responsible for secretion and absorption in the mucosa.
This class serves as a reference for healthy glandular tissue in the gastrointestinal tract.
simple_stroma: Histology image showing simple stroma, composed of loose connective tissue with few cells and abundant extracellular matrix.
Simple stroma appears as pale, fibrous areas with scattered fibroblasts and minimal cellularity.
The image may reveal thin collagen fibers and occasional blood vessels within the stroma.
Simple stroma provides structural support for glands and other tissue elements.
This class is characterized by its uniform, uncomplicated appearance.
tumour_epithelium: Histology image showing tumour epithelium, composed of malignant epithelial cells forming irregular nests or sheets.
Tumour epithelium appears as crowded, pleomorphic cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and increased mitotic activity.
The image may reveal loss of normal glandular architecture and evidence of invasion into surrounding stroma.
Tumour epithelium is a hallmark of carcinoma and is associated with aggressive growth.
This class is defined by the presence of abnormal, proliferating epithelial cells.
